crumbs: Computational Fluid Mechanics and Heat Transfer

title: Chapter 4. Application of Numerical Methods to Model Equations
text:
The Burgers’ equation serves as an analog to the Navier Stokes equations. The equation includes an unsteady term, a nonlinear convection term, and a viscous term.
\[
\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}+u\frac {\partial u}{\partial x}=\mu\frac {\partial^2u}{\partial x^2}
\tag{1}\]
When the viscous term is included, equation (1) is parabolic. The inviscid Burgers’ equation arises when viscous effects are zero, \(\mu=0\).
\[
\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}+u\frac {\partial u}{\partial x}=0
\tag{2}\]
Equation (2) may be viewed as a nonlinear wave equation where each point on the wave front can propagate at different speeds. The linear, 1D convection equation considered earlier is a simpler case of this, where the propagation speed of all waves was at a constant speed. Since Burgers’ equation contains varying wave speeds, shocks may be modeled due to the coalescing of wave information.
Nonlinear hyperbolic PDEs exhibit two types of solutions according to Lax (1954). Consider the general case of the equation below. Introduce the Jacobian \(A=\frac {\partial F_i}{\partial u_j}\) from the chain rule.
\[
\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}+\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}=\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}+A\frac {\partial u}{\partial x}=0
\tag{3}\]


First order methods are rarely used in solving hyperbolic equations owing to their dissipative nature. The Lax method is typically used to demonstrate its dissipative nature and the application of a first order method to a nonlinear equation.
\[
u_j^{n+1}=\frac {u_{j-1}^n+u_{j+1}^n}2-\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}\frac {F_{j+1}^n-F_{j-1}^n}2
\tag{4}\]
The first term is \(u_j^n\) averaged between its neighboring cells. This introduces artificial viscosity into the method that smooths out any instabilities or oscillations in the solution.






NoteDerivation of the Lax Method





Expand \(u_j^{n+1}\) about \(u_j^n\) using a Taylor series and substitute the time derivative term with the spatial derivative. From equation (3), we have that \(u_t=-F_x\). Therefore
\[
\begin{align*}
u(x,t+\Delta t) & =u(x,t)+\left.\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}\right|_{x,t}\Delta t+\left.\frac {\partial^2u}{\partial t^2}\right|_{x,t}\frac {\Delta t^2}{2!}+\cdots \\
& =u(x, t)-\left.\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right|_{x,t}\Delta t+\mathcal O(\Delta t^2)
\end{align*}
\]
Neglecting the higher order terms and applying a central difference scheme on \(F_x\), the Lax method is obtained. An average is used for calculating the \(u_j^n\) term to introduce numerical dissipation.
\[u_j^{n+1}=u_j^n-\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}\frac {F_{j+1}^n-F_{j-1}^n}2\]



The amplification factor for this scheme is given below, where \(\beta=k\Delta x\) and \(A=\frac {\partial F}{\partial u}\). Since \(F=\frac {u^2}2\) for Burgers’ equation, \(A=u\).
\[
G=\cos\beta-i\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}A\sin\beta
\tag{5}\]
Stability occurs when \(|G|\leq1\) and equation (5) reduces down to
\[
\left|\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}u_{\mathrm{max}}\right|\leq1
\]
where \(u_{\mathrm{max}}\) is the maximum eigenvalue of the Jacobian \(A\).



The Lax-Wendroff method was one of the first second order finite difference methods derived for hyperbolic PDEs. Once again, we consider the general form of the governing equation (3). The Lax-Wendroff method is
\[
u_j^{n+1}=u_j^n-\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}\frac {F_{j+1}^n-F_{j-1}^n}2+\frac 12\left(\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}\right)^2\left[A_{j+\frac 12}^n\left(F_{j+1}^n-F_j^n\right)-A_{j-\frac 12}^n\left(F_j^n-F_{j-1}^n\right)\right]
\tag{6}\]
There are many different ways of evaluating the Jacobian matrix at the half interval \(j\pm\frac 12\). For Lax-Wendroff, one way is to use a simple average of the neighboring cells. Since \(A=A(u)\), then we have
\[
A_{j\pm\frac 12}=A\left(\frac {u_j+u_{j\pm1}}2\right)
\]
For Burgers’ equation, \(A(u)=u\) so the half interval Jacobians reduce down to
\[
\begin{align*}
A_{j+\frac 12} & =\frac {u_j+u_{j+1}}2 \\
A_{j-\frac 12} & =\frac {u_{j-1}+u_j}2
\end{align*}
\]






NoteDerivation of the Lax-Wendroff Method





Start off in the same manner as the Lax method and expand \(u+j^{n+1}\) about \(u_j^n\) using a Taylor series.
\[
u(x,t+\Delta t)=u(x,t)+\left.\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}\right|_{x,t}\Delta t+\left.\frac {\partial^2u}{\partial t^2}\right|_{x,t}\frac {\Delta t^2}{2!}+\left.\frac {\partial^3u}{\partial t^3}\right|_{x,t}\frac {\Delta t^3}{3!}+\cdots
\]
Next, replace the time derivatives with spatial derivatives. From equation (3), the first time derivative is
\[
\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}=-\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}
\]
For the second derivative term, differentiate the equation above with respect to time and interchange the differentiation order.
\[
\frac {\partial^2u}{\partial t^2}=-\frac {\partial}{\partial x}\frac {\partial F}{\partial t}=-\frac {\partial}{\partial x}\frac {\partial F}{\partial u}\frac {\partial u}{\partial t}=\frac {\partial}{\partial x}\left(A\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right)
\]
The last step is due to the chain rule. Substitute into the Taylor series to obtain
\[
u(x,t+\Delta t)=u(x,t)-\left.\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right|_{x,t}\Delta t+\left.\frac {\partial}{\partial x}\left(A\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right)\right|_{x,t}\frac {\Delta t^2}2+\mathcal O(\Delta t^3)
\]
Apply central differencing schemes to all derivative terms. The second order derivative is
\[
\begin{align*}
\frac {\partial}{\partial x}\left(A\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right) & \approx\frac {A_{j+\frac 12}\left.\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right|_{j+\frac 12}-A_{j-\frac 12}\left.\frac {\partial F}{\partial x}\right|_{j-\frac 12}}{\Delta x} \\ & =\frac 1{\Delta x}\left[A_{j+\frac 12}\frac {F_{j+1}-F_j}{\Delta x}-A_{j-\frac 12}\frac {F_j-F_{j-1}}{\Delta x}\right] \\ & =\frac 1{\Delta x^2}\left[A_{j+\frac 12}\left(F_{j+1}-F_j\right)-A_{j-\frac 12}\left(F_j-F_{j-1}\right)\right]
\end{align*}
\]
The \(\partial F/\partial x\) terms when evaluated at the cell interface utilize a central difference, as \(F_j\) and \(F_{j\pm1}\) are both exactly \(\frac {\Delta x}2\) away from the boundary. Therefore, central differences are used to decompose all derivative terms into their stencil approximations and the second order accuracy is maintained. Putting everything together gives the Lax-Wendroff method.



The amplification factor is
\[
G=1-2\left(\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}A\right)^2(1-\cos\beta)-2i\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}A\sin\beta
\tag{7}\]
When \(|G|\leq1\), the scheme is stable. For the amplification above, this simplifies down to \(\left|\frac {\Delta t}{\Delta x}u_{\mathrm{max}}\right|\leq1\). When Lax-Wendroff is applied to the example problem, the method correctly predicts the position of the discontinuity. Moreover, the solution is relatively sharp and well-defined, with some oscillations occurring at either ends of the discontinuity. In comparison, the Lax method was purely monotone and did not exhibit any oscillatory motion. As the Courant number is decreased, the number of oscillations in the Lax-Wendroff solution increases as the solution quality is degraded.
